did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (CNN ensemble) and machine learning (SVM ensemble) models to perform skin cancer detection and classification on the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is presented at the 11th International Conference on Bioinformatics and Computational Biology (ICBCB) and reports original experimental results. It details the methodology of comparing CNN and SVM ensemble models on the HAM10000 dataset, including data preprocessing, experimental setup, and performance evaluation metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity). The abstract and conclusion explicitly state that the study's contribution is the production of a comparative study of these models and that the CNN ensemble performed better, indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: A Comparative Study of Ensemble Deep Learning Models for Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2023 11th International Conference on Bioinformatics and Computational Biology (ICBCB)
brief_summary: This study compares the performance of CNN and SVM ensemble models for multi-class skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset of 10,015 dermatoscopic images. The researchers employed a one-versus-rest approach to decompose the classification problem into binary tasks, training separate models for each of the seven lesion categories. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and computational complexity, with the dataset split into 80% training and 20% testing sets. The results demonstrated that the CNN ensemble model outperformed the SVM ensemble in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. Additionally, the CNN approach was significantly more efficient, requiring only 25 minutes of training time and a 63MB model size compared to the SVM's 6 hours and 10.46GB. The study concludes that the CNN ensemble is superior for skin lesion detection due to its higher predictive performance and lower computational resource requirements.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (7 classes: Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The study decomposes the multi-class problem into binary classification tasks using a one-versus-rest (OVR) approach, training separate binary classifiers for each of the seven lesion categories. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (class labels) rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A diverse collection of multi-sourced dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, provided by the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna in Austria and Cliff Rosendahl's skin cancer clinic in Queensland, Australia. The dataset consists of 10,015 images.

use.training: Used for training the CNN and SVM ensemble models. The dataset was split into an 80% training set (8000 samples) and a 20% testing set (2000 samples). The multi-class problem was decomposed into binary classification tasks using a one-versus-rest approach.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the trained models. The testing set contained 2000 samples.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the CNN and SVM ensemble models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, training time, model size) without statistical significance testing.

SVM Ensemble.architecture: Seven binary classifiers using Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel with regularization parameter C=1.0

CNN Ensemble.architecture: Seven binary classifiers using three convolutional layers and three fully connected layers, Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.001, batch size 64, 10 epochs

HAM10000.notes: The CNN ensemble model outperformed the SVM ensemble model in terms of accuracy, sensitivity (TPR), and specificity (TNR). The CNN ensemble had a significantly lower training time (25 minutes vs 6 hours) and smaller model size (63MB vs 10.46GB). The SVM ensemble showed very low sensitivity for classes 5 and 6 (TPR=0), indicating a high risk of false negatives for those classes.

architecture_used.main_model: CNN Ensemble (One-Versus-Rest approach) and SVM Ensemble

CNN_Engine.layers: Three convolutional layers and three fully connected layers
CNN_Engine.ensemble_method: One-Versus-Rest (OVR) with seven binary classifiers stacked via a custom PyTorch-based class
CNN_Engine.loss_handling: Class weights adjusted while computing loss to address dataset imbalance

SVM_Engine.ensemble_method: One-Versus-Rest (OVR) with seven binary classifiers stacked using stackingClassifier class from mlextend library

training_details.hardware: GPU (private Kaggle kernel) for CNN; Local system (Intel Core i5 4-core CPU, 8GB RAM, 2.3GHz) for SVM

classification_or_output_head.CNN: Seven binary classifiers output stacked and run through three additional fully connected layers to create the ensemble model for final prediction
classification_or_output_head.SVM: Seven binary classifiers; final classification chosen as the class with the highest output from the binary classifiers

preprocessing_techniques: Elimination of damaged or unused data
Removal of cases with unclear diagnoses based on histopathologic diagnosis
Manual correction of histograms for underexposed photographs or those with clear yellow or green tint by a skilled dermatologist
Color illuminant estimation under the grey-world assumption
Normalization of pixel values to achieve zero mean and unit variance
conclusions: The CNN ensemble model outperformed the SVM ensemble model in terms of accuracy, sensitivity (TPR), and specificity (TNR) for multi-class skin cancer detection on the HAM10000 dataset.

The CNN ensemble model demonstrated significantly superior computational efficiency compared to the SVM ensemble, requiring only 25 minutes of training time and resulting in a model size of 63 MB, whereas the SVM ensemble required 6 hours of training and resulted in a model size of 10.46 GB.

The SVM ensemble model exhibited very low sensitivity (TPR) for Dermatofibroma (Class 5) and Vascular Lesion (Class 6), with a TPR of 0, indicating a high risk of false negatives for these specific classes.
The CNN ensemble model achieved an overall sensitivity of 0.712 and specificity of 0.9581, which the authors note is a wide margin improvement over the SVM ensemble's sensitivity of 0.166.

The study acknowledges that the results are based on a specific dataset (HAM10000) and may not generalize to other datasets, highlighting the need for further research using different datasets and evaluation measures.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which has known population biases (images from high-risk melanoma patients in Austria and severe sun damage patients in Australia), limiting generalizability to broader dermatological populations.

The dataset is highly imbalanced, with Melanocytic Nevus (6705 samples) dominating while Dermatofibroma (115 samples) and Vascular Lesion (142 samples) are severely underrepresented, potentially skewing model performance.

The SVM ensemble model exhibited critically low sensitivity (TPR) of 0 for Dermatofibroma (Class 5) and Vascular Lesion (Class 6), indicating a complete failure to detect these classes and a high risk of false negatives.

The CNN ensemble model achieved an overall sensitivity of 0.712, which, while superior to SVM, still leaves a significant portion of positive cases undetected, posing clinical safety concerns for a diagnostic tool.
No external validation was performed; results are based solely on an 80/20 split of the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and robustness on unseen data.
The study lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the reported performance differences between the CNN and SVM ensembles.

The CNN model was trained on a private Kaggle GPU, while the SVM was trained on a local CPU (Intel Core i5, 8GB RAM), introducing hardware heterogeneity that may affect reproducibility and fair comparison of computational efficiency.

The paper does not report results for individual binary classifiers, only the aggregated ensemble performance, making it difficult to assess the contribution of specific lesion types to the overall metrics.
The study acknowledges that results may not generalize to other datasets, highlighting a limitation in the external validity of the proposed ensemble methods.
